# Deploying to Render (and/or Netlify) 
#deploymentAndDelivery #theWalkingSkeleton

_Last updated: Sept 20th, 2024_

_Topics: render, Netlify, environment variables, deployment secrets, provisioned services_

_Major Topics: Architectural Components, Deployment Pipelines_

Now that we’ve got our Postgres instance set up, we’re unblocked to deploy.

By the end of this lesson, you can officially say you’ve created a _Minimum Deployment Pipeline_. But it’s just the start. We’ll continue to improve by adding phases in between.

## Lesson Goals

- _What is a minimum deployment pipeline & what does it have to do with the walking skeleton?_
- _The deployment process_
- _How to deploy?_

## What is a minimum deployment pipeline again?

We are now ready to deploy our application to a Minimum Deployment Pipeline.

What is that again?

Basically, it’s the pipeline of phases that run your tests, your deployments, your checks, before deploying all the way to production.

![](data:image/webp;base64,)

_Pictured above is a very good deployment pipeline. We won’t do all of the above, but it’s a good setup to shoot for._

### Relationship to The Walking Skeleton

Now, in this course, what did we do?

We built the frontend. We built the backend. We added tests onto the frontend and backend after the fact.

All cool, in terms of the _learning process_, but in **real life, you should actually start in reverse**. **In real life, you should actually start by deploying.**

Why?

We’ve talked about this already, but to reiterate — look at the common theme in _Best Practice-First_.

You _start at the end_. That’s how you get what you want.

By starting at the end — ie: deploying an entire Vertical Slice and setting up all of the infrastructure, scripts, architecture, tests, and so on — you **create the highest possible likelihood that you’ll succeed**.

When we push deployment off to the very end, like we’re doing in this training material, it actually makes things harder.

So make sure you take this away: **start by deploying a Walking Skeleton comprised of a single vertical slice**, because everything we’ll do here and in the following lessons in this submodule, it can get pretty crazy if left till the very end, after you’ve built out a ton of features.

## Deployment concerns

There can be a LOT of different concerns in the deployment process.

For example, here’s a short list of things we’re addressing:

- _**Continuous Integration Server (running the pipeline stages)**_
- _**Backend Deployment Platform (hosting the backend)**_
- _**Frontend Deployment Platform (hosting the frontend)**_
- _**Other services to deploy (databases, caches, queues, etc)**_
- _**Environment or Deploy Secrets Management (passing environment variables into the frontend and/or backend at deploy/runtime)**_
- _**Migrations (migrating the database)**_
- _**Automatic or Manual Deploys to Production?**_

In our submission, we’ve chosen to use Render, but you’re free to use whatever you’d like.

This will involve adjusting configurations, managing environment variables, and making sure your scripts work properly.

Let’s go ahead and get started.

## 1. Creating your infrastructure in Render

First thing is first — I’m using _Render_ to deploy everything.

Why? It’s straightforward.

Signing up for the service, I’ve opted to create the following services:

- _frontend_
- _db_
- _backend_

Take a look at the following screenshot to see what yours should look like.

![](data:image/webp;base64,)

If you haven’t already, sign up for the Render, set up the services, and make sure you can access each of them.

## 2. Environment variables (frontend to backend)

Now to get them all talking to each other. At the moment, because we haven’t properly set up testing or production variables, when you visit the frontend via the URL, you should be getting an error like the following:

![](data:image/webp;base64,)

To fix this, let’s make a few changes.

### **2.1. Local setup for development mode .env.development (VITE_API_URL)**.

First of all, let’s add a new environment variable to the frontend to represent how we’ll use the app locally. We can start with the .env.development environment variable. Note that we must use _VITE_APP__ as the prefix to any environment variables so long as we use Vite to build our React apps.

packages/frontend/.env.development

```yaml
VITE_API_URL=http://localhost:3000
```

Next, let’s create a config object we can use to pass this around.

packages/frontend/src/config.tsx

```tsx
export const appConfig = {
  apiURL: import.meta.env.VITE_API_URL
}
```

And now we can make use of the frontend config as we hand it off to the apiClient.

packages/frontend/src/App.tsx

```tsx
import "./App.css";
import "react-toastify/dist/ReactToastify.css";
import { createAPIClient } from '@dddforum/shared/src/api'

import { BrowserRouter } from "react-router-dom";
import { Route, Routes } from "react-router-dom";
import { MainPage } from "./pages/mainPage";
import { RegisterPage } from "./pages/registerPage";
import { UserProvider } from "./contexts/userContext";
import { SpinnerProvider } from "./contexts/spinnerContext";
import { appConfig } from "./config";

export const api = createAPIClient(appConfig.apiURL);

function App() {
  return (
    <SpinnerProvider>
      <UserProvider>
        <BrowserRouter>
          <meta name="color-scheme" content="light only"></meta>
          <Routes>
            <Route path="/" element={<MainPage />} />
            <Route path="/join" element={<RegisterPage />} />
          </Routes>
        </BrowserRouter>
      </UserProvider>
    </SpinnerProvider>
  );
}

export default App;
```

### **2.2. Update in render’s environment variables for production mode (VITE_API_URL)**.

Once we’ve confirmed that we can still reach the backend locally via our new env variable config, let’s set it up in Render.

In the environments tab, you can enter the URL of your backend API url (also in Render)

![](data:image/webp;base64,)

### **2.3. Confirm it works**.

Once you’ve set these up and re-built the frontend, make sure you can reach the backend by going to the URL and clicking around.

## 3. Environment variables (backend to database)

### **3.1. Confirm the local environment variables work**.

I always like to take a look at the local environment variable and configuration first before I set it up in the cloud.

_Thanks to Kirill (karpov_kir), we’re using a `prepareEnv` script which helps us load the correct .env file into the various scripts we run in sequence. For example, to do a prisma migrate, we also have to make sure the env has been prepared with the correct variables. And in production, we don’t want to read from .env files, so there was a fair amount of complexity here to encapsulate. This tidy little script helps us load the development environment by default._

_packages/backend/prepareEnv.ts_

```tsx
/**
 * This script allows you to call any other following script with
 * `ts-node prepareEnv <whatever you want to call next>` and if your app
 * is running in development mode (not no NODE_ENV set at all, assumed), it
 * will load the env file before you call the script. This loads the environment
 * up properly.
 * 
 * We currently need this for prisma commands to allow prisma to take the config from
 * .env.development in development mode, and from the secrets in the deployment tools in
 * production.
 */

import { execSync } from 'child_process';
import path from 'path';

export const prepareEnv = (): void => {
  let env = process.env.NODE_ENV || 'development';
  const packageRoot = path.resolve(__dirname);
  const execParams = {
    cwd: packageRoot,
    stdio: 'inherit',
  } as const;

  let script = process.argv.splice(2).join(' ');

  if (env === 'development') {
    let devEnvFile = '.env.development'
    console.log(`Preparing dev environment using ${devEnvFile}`);
    execSync(`dotenv -e ${devEnvFile} -- ${script}`, execParams);
    return;
  }

  console.log(`Running ${script} in ${process.env.NODE_ENV} mode without loading from env file.`);
  execSync(`${script}`, execParams);
  
};

prepareEnv();
```

And notice that we call upon this sub-script for the majority of our npm scripts.

```json
{
  "name": "@dddforum/backend",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "The backend for dddforum.",
  "main": "index.js",
  "scripts": {
    "lint": "eslint \\"**/src/**/*.ts\\"",
    "clean": "rimraf ./dist",
    "build": "tsc -b tsconfig.json && npm run generate --schema=./src/shared/database/prisma/schema.prisma",
    "generate": "ts-node prepareEnv.ts prisma generate --schema=./src/shared/database/prisma/schema.prisma",
    "migrate": "ts-node prepareEnv.ts prisma migrate dev --schema=./src/shared/database/prisma/schema.prisma",
    "db:seed": "ts-node prepareEnv.ts prisma db seed --schema=./src/shared/database/prisma/schema.prism",
    "db:reset": "ts-node prepareEnv.ts prisma migrate reset --preview-feature --schema src/shared/database/prisma/schema.prisma && npm run migrate && npm run generate",
    "start:dev": "npm run migrate && npm run generate && npx nodemon",
    "start:dev:no-watch": "npm run migrate && npm run generate && ts-node prepareEnv.ts ts-node src/index.ts",
    "start": "npm run migrate && npm run db:seed && node build/index.js",
    "test": "jest",
    "test:dev": "jest --watchAll",
    "test:e2e": "jest -c jest.config.e2e.ts",
    "test:e2e:dev": "jest -c jest.config.e2e.ts --watch",
    "start:db:dev": "docker-compose up -d",
    "test:infra": "jest -c jest.config.infra.ts",
    "test:infra:dev": "jest -c jest.config.infra.ts --watch"
  },
  "prisma": {
    "seed": "ts-node prisma/seed.ts"
  },
  "keywords": [],
  "author": "",
  "license": "ISC",
  "devDependencies": {
    "@types/cors": "^2.8.17",
    "@types/express": "^4.17.17",
    "@types/nodemailer": "^6.4.14",
    "dotenv": "^16.3.1",
    "dotenv-cli": "^7.3.0",
    "nodemon": "^2.0.19"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "@dddforum/shared": "file:../shared",
    "@mailchimp/mailchimp_marketing": "^3.0.80",
    "@prisma/client": "^4.16.0",
    "cors": "^2.8.5",
    "express": "^4.18.2",
    "nodemailer": "^6.9.7",
    "pg": "^8.11.0",
    "prisma": "^4.16.0"
  }
}

```

### **3.2. Set up database url in render**.

Do the same thing for the backend. It needs access to the database, so put the DATABASE_URL in as one of the environment variables.

Copy the _Internal Database URL_.

![](data:image/webp;base64,)

And paste that in as the DATABASE_URL.

![](data:image/webp;base64,)

### **3.3. Adjust scripts to seed the database as well (if it doesn’t)**

You’ll also want to see some data in your staging environment, initially.

You can make use of a seed script:

```tsx
import { User, Post, Vote, Comment, PrismaClient } from "@prisma/client";

const prisma = new PrismaClient();

const initialUsers: User[] = [
  {
    id: 1,
    email: "bobvance@gmail.com",
    firstName: "Bob",
    lastName: "Vance",
    username: "bobvance",
    password: '123'
  },
  {
    id: 2,
    email: "tonysoprano@gmail.com",
    firstName: "Tony",
    lastName: "Soprano",
    username: "tonysoprano",
    password: '123'
  },
  {
    id: 3,
    email: "billburr@gmail.com",
    firstName: "Bill",
    lastName: "Burr",
    username: "billburr",
    password: '123'
  },
];

const initialPosts: Post[] = [
  {
    id: 1,
    title: 'First post!',
    content: "This is bob vances first post",
    postType: "Text",
    dateCreated: new Date(),
    memberId: 1,
  },
  {
    id: 2,
    title: 'Second post!',
    content: "This is bobs second post",
    postType: "Text",
    dateCreated: new Date(),
    memberId: 1,
  },
  {
    id: 3,
    title: 'another post',
    content: "This is tonys first post",
    postType: "Text",
    dateCreated: new Date(),
    memberId: 2,
  },
  {
    id: 4,
    title: 'Links',
    content: "This is a link post",
    postType: "<https://khalilstemmler.com>",
    dateCreated: new Date(),
    memberId: 2,
  },
];

const initialPostVotes: Vote[] = [
  // Everyone upvotes their own first post
  { id: 1, postId: 1, voteType: 'Upvote', memberId: 1 },
  { id: 2, postId: 2, voteType: 'Upvote', memberId: 1 },
  { id: 3, postId: 3, voteType: 'Upvote', memberId: 2 },
  { id: 4, postId: 4, voteType: 'Upvote', memberId: 2 },

  // Tony's post upvoted by Bob
  { id: 5, postId: 3, voteType: 'Upvote', memberId: 1 },

  // Bob's second post downvoted by Bill
  { id: 6, postId: 2, voteType: 'Downvote', memberId: 3 },
];

const initialPostComments: Comment[] = [
  { id: 1, text: 'I posted this!', memberId: 1, postId: 1, parentCommentId: null },
  { id: 2, text: 'Nice', memberId: 2, postId: 2, parentCommentId: null }
];

async function seed() {

  let totalPostsCount = await prisma.post.count();
  if (totalPostsCount !== 0) {
    console.log('Already seeded.')
    return;
  }

  try {
    for (const user of initialUsers) {
      const newUser = await prisma.user.create({
        data: user
      });
  
      await prisma.member.create({
        data: {
          user: {
            connect: { id: newUser.id },
          },
        },
      });
    }
  
    for (const post of initialPosts) {
      await prisma.post.create({
        data: post,
      });
    }
  
    for (const vote of initialPostVotes) {
      await prisma.vote.create({
        data: vote,
      });
    }
  
    for (const comment of initialPostComments) {
      await prisma.comment.create({
        data: comment,
      });
    }
  } catch (err) {
    console.log(err)
  }
}

seed();

```

And then update your run script.

```yaml
"start": "npm run migrate && npm run db:seed && node build/index.js",
```

### **3.4. Test frontend to backend**

Now go to the frontend URL and verify that you can reach the backend.

## Your Turn!

- 🔘 _I have deployed my frontend to either to Render or Netlify_
- 🔘 _I have deployed my backend to either to Render or Netlify_
- 🔘 _I have set up and connected my backend to a Postgres database with Render_
- 🔘 _I can visit my frontend and successfully register from the URL of the frontend_

If you have any questions or suggestions to improve this lesson, leave a comment below.

As always, To Mastery.
